parts.text:
Here is a rough approximation of what happened. Change whatever feels wrong — the events, the details, the shape of it. It's just a starting point.

You are clearly not okay. Not okay to the point that everyone around you can see it, but nobody dares say anything because what would they even say. The way you express your inner thoughts — or try to — should reflect that. Not as a style choice. Just because that's where you are.

---

It’s 7:56 AM. Thursday. 

I am looking at my phone screen. The brightness is turned all the way down because light hurts my eyes lately, everything hurts my eyes, everything is a little too sharp. 

*Hey! Good luck on the bio exam today, you’ve been studying so hard for it. You’re gonna do great. Get a coffee first!* 

I hit send to Chloe. 

My thumb is shaking. Just a fine, tiny tremor, right at the joint. I watch it vibrate against the glass of the screen. I put the phone face down on the counter. 

I open the cabinet. I take out the mugs. The yellow one for Maya. The big blue one for me. I put the kettle on. I do this because it is morning, and Maya is sleeping in the next room, and when she wakes up she will want tea. I do this because I am Riley, and this is what Riley does. 

If I stop doing what Riley does, I don’t know who is left. 

The kettle starts to hiss. Inside my chest, there is a block of concrete. It is so heavy I can barely draw a full breath around it. It sits right behind my sternum, pressing, pressing, crushing the air out. 

A party. 
The music thumping through the floorboards. 
The smell of sweat and cheap citrus vodka.
The way the door clicked shut. 
*Wait.* I said. *Wait, hold on.*
He didn’t stop. 

I grip the edge of the kitchen counter. The laminate is cold. I press my palms into it until the edges bite into my skin, just to feel something that is on the outside of my body instead of the inside. 

He didn’t stop. I said it. I know I said it. 
*Please.* 
Just a heavy weight. Pushing me down. Pinning my wrists. 
The music thumping. 
No one heard. No one knew. 

The kettle whistles. I jump, a violent, full-body flinch that knocks my hip against the drawer. I grab the handle, my heart hammering a frantic, sick rhythm against the concrete block in my chest. I pour the water. Chamomile for Maya. Earl grey for me. 

"Morning." 

Maya is in the doorway. She looks sleepy. Her hair is a mess. I love her so much it physically aches, I have loved her since we were twelve years old eating pizza rolls on her basement floor in Grand Rapids. 

"Morning," I say. My voice sounds perfectly normal. It terrifies me how normal it sounds. "Tea’s ready."

"You're an angel," she mumbles, shuffling over. She reaches for the yellow mug. As she takes it, her fingers brush mine. 

I pull back. I can't help it. It’s like touching a hot stove. I jerk my hand away so fast tea sloshes over the rim of the mug and burns my knuckles. 

Maya freezes. She looks at my hand. Then she looks at my face. 

The silence stretches. It’s thick. It’s awful. 

"Ry?" she says softly. 

"Sorry," I say quickly. I grab a paper towel. I wipe the counter. I wipe the mug. I don't look at her. "Static shock. You know. Dry air."

She doesn’t move. She just watches me wipe the counter. I can feel her looking at me. She knows. She doesn't know *what*, but she knows. For three weeks now, she has been watching me from across rooms. I catch her looking at me when she thinks I don’t see. Her eyes are full of this awful, helpless grief. She sees the glass wall between us. She presses her hands flat against it, but she can't break it. If she breaks it, the vacuum will pull her in. If she asks me what's wrong, if she really pushes, I will shatter into a million jagged pieces on the kitchen floor and neither of us will know how to put me back together.

So she takes the mug. "Thanks, Ry."

"Of course."

I smile. It uses every muscle in my face. 

It's 8:04 AM. Chloe texts back: *omg u remembered! thank u literally crying rn 😭*

I heart the message. 

Later, it's evening. The Whitmore group is coming over. Six people. My people. My small, safe world. 

I make a grocery run. I buy the cheddar popcorn David likes. I buy the exact brand of seltzer Sarah drinks. I carry the bags up the stairs. My legs feel like lead. I am so tired. It is a tiredness that sleep does not touch. I sleep for nine hours and wake up exhausted. I wake up in the middle of the night and the concrete block is there, pressing me into the mattress. I wake up remembering the feeling of breath on my neck and I go to the bathroom and vomit up nothing but stomach acid. 

I arrange the snacks in bowls. I put a soft, knit blanket over the back of the sofa. I turn off the overhead light and turn on the three lamps. Warm light. Amber light. Safe. 

I create the conditions for ease. I build a little terrarium of comfort and I put my friends inside it. 

They arrive. They laugh. They drop their coats on my bed. 

I queue up a playlist. Mellow indie, warm acoustics, a little bit of R&B. Seamless. 

"Dude, the vibes in here are always so immaculate," David says, sinking into the sofa and grabbing a handful of popcorn. "I swear my blood pressure drops ten points when I walk in."

"I aim to please," I say. 

I sit in the armchair. I pull my knees to my chest. I wrap my arms around my shins. 

They are talking. Sarah is complaining about her lab partner. Maya is laughing. The room is glowing, exactly the way I made it to glow. 

I am not in the room. 

I am floating somewhere near the ceiling, looking down at my own body. Or I am back in that dark bedroom at the party, staring up at a ceiling I don't recognize. The smell of the vodka. The bruising grip on my hips. The absolute, terrifying realization that my voice did not matter. That I was a thing. Just a warm thing to be used. 

*I am a person,* I think desperately, sitting in my armchair. *I am Riley.* 

I watch David’s cup. When it gets low, I get up and hand him a new seltzer. He doesn't even have to ask. 

"Thanks, Ry." 

"Gotcha."

I sit back down. I am a ghost haunting my own life. I am a machine made of soft sweaters and good playlists and perfectly timed text messages. Inside, it is just wreckage. Torn metal and smoking ruins and silence. Nobody can hear it. I am screaming so loud my throat is bleeding, but the acoustics in the room are perfect, the lighting is flawless, the popcorn is exactly right. 

Sunday. 

The phone buzzes on my nightstand. 

Mom. 

I stare at it. It buzzes and buzzes. It vibrates against the wood. 

I have to answer. I call her every Sunday. If I don't answer, she will worry. 

I clear my throat. I swallow the jagged glass in my throat. 

"Hi, Mom."

"Hi, sweetie! How was your week?"

"Good," I say. I am lying flat on my back. I have the heavy quilt pulled up to my chin. I used to love the weight of it. Now it feels like a body. I push it off. I am shivering. I pull it back up. "Just busy. Classes."

"You sound a little quiet, honey. Are you getting enough sleep?"

"Yeah. Just studying a lot."

"Well, make sure you take a break, okay? Have you seen Maya?"

"She's right in the living room."

"Good. Tell her I say hi. And Riley?"

"Yeah?"

"I love you."

I squeeze my eyes shut. A tear slips out. It runs hot into my hair. It is the only warm thing inside me. 

"I love you too, Mom."

I hang up. I hold the phone to my chest. 

I want my mom. I want my mom to drive the three hours from home and sit on my bed and rub my back. But if she comes, she will look at me, and she will see it. Mothers always see it. And if she sees it, it becomes real. The wreckage comes out of my chest and spills all over the floor and ruins her too. 

I can't ruin them. Any of them. 

So I hold it. 

I hold the concrete. I hold the broken glass. I hold the memory of the weight, the smell, the terrible, suffocating dark. I pack it down tight, deep inside, underneath the warmth. 

I get out of bed. I walk into the living room. 

Maya is studying on the couch. The light from the window is hitting her screen weirdly. 

I reach over and adjust the blinds. The glare disappears. 

She looks up at me. Those sad, helpless eyes. 

"Better?" I ask. 

"Yeah," she whispers. "Better."

I go to the kitchen. I check the water filter. It's almost empty. 

I fill it. I put it in the fridge. I keep going. 

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

**My Approach to Refining the Draft**

Okay, so here's how I'm thinking about tackling this revision. First, I'm really honing in on the core of Riley. She's supposed to be inherently warm, this person who naturally creates ease, right? Not some performance, but something deep-seated. That's key. The goal isn't to *show* her acting, it's to highlight this intrinsic warmth. She's not hiding behind it; it's just what she does. And the trauma? It's disrupting that. It's the *contrast* that's going to hit hardest.

The draft needs work. It's too explicit. Riley wouldn't narrate her own trauma with such polished language. It's clunky, too much *telling* when we should be *showing*. It’s a sensory experience, so that's where I need to focus. I'm thinking fragmented images, jarring details. The flashback? Needs to feel visceral, less like a constructed scene. More like intrusive, unwanted memories.

I’m breaking down the scenes. Morning/texting: Keep the body language and start with a sensory intrusion. Making tea: Ditch the meta-commentary, focus on the physical action. Trauma memory: Short, sharp, sensory. The objectification has to be overwhelming. The details of the counter vs. the bed, the feeling of not being a person.

With Maya, the flinch and the burnt knuckles are gold. I just need to make the distance *felt*, not narrated. Riley can't know what Maya is thinking; she just senses the chasm. The party/hosting is where it gets interesting: the dissociation. It's autopilot, but alien. Instead of "I am a machine," it's more "I'm watching my hand hand David a seltzer... It's a nice hand... It's not mine."

The phone call with her mom? Keep the urge to tell simple, the conflict between that and the need to protect her mom. And the ending... the endless loop. Fixing the blinds for Maya. Filling the pitcher. It's quiet.

The voice has to be right. A little disjointed when the trauma hits, smooth when she's in her "Riley" mode. Sensory details are crucial – touch, light, temperature. Comfort is now corrupted. That weight of the blanket, ugh. She's not *acting* like she cares about David; she *does* care, but the care doesn't fill the void.

I'm making a list to refine specific lines, too. Like, "I build a little terrarium of comfort" is too poetic. It’s better to show it by just talking about turning off a bright light and filling the room with lamps.

For those scenes, I was thinking about how empty it is now inside. No "wreckage" – it’s a feeling of absence.

The final polish is a rhythmic check and makes sure Maya's reaction feels grounded and true. The transitions need to hit hard: The warmth and the shattering. Let’s make it real.

This is incredibly close. The structure, the moments you’ve chosen, the devastating contrast between her external actions and internal state—it’s all there. 

The only thing that needs shifting is the narration. In the original draft, Riley is explaining her trauma, using metaphors like "terrariums" and "machines" and "wreckage." But when you are in the thick of it, you aren't a poet about it. You don't analyze your own dissociation. You just *experience* it. The horror of what she's going through will actually hit much harder if we strip away the explanations and just let her exist in the sensory nightmare of it.

Here is a revision that brings the camera closer into her head, focusing on the terrible, quiet autopilot of being Riley when Riley has been hollowed out.

***

It’s 7:56 AM. Thursday.

My phone screen is turned all the way down. Light hurts my eyes lately. Everything hurts my eyes. Everything in the apartment feels a little too sharp, like the edges of the furniture have been filed into blades.

*Hey! Good luck on the bio exam today, you’ve been studying so hard for it. You’re gonna do great. Get a coffee first!*

I hit send to Chloe.

My thumb is shaking. Just a fine, tiny tremor right at the joint. I watch it vibrate against the glass. I put the phone face down on the counter so I don't have to look at it.

I open the cabinet. I take out the mugs. The yellow one for Maya. The big blue one for me. I put the kettle on. I do this because it is morning, and Maya is sleeping in the next room, and when she wakes up she will want tea. 

The kettle starts to hiss. 

I stare at the burner. The coils are turning red. 

Inside my chest, there is something thick and heavy. I can’t draw a full breath around it. It sits right behind my sternum, pressing, crushing the air out.

A closed door.
The bass from downstairs rattling the floorboards.
The smell of somebody else's sweat.
*Wait.* I said it. I know I said it. *Wait, hold on.*

I grip the edge of the kitchen counter. The laminate is cold. I press my palms into it until the edge bites into my skin, just to feel something that is on the outside of my body. 

He didn’t stop. 
*Please.*
The terrible, suffocating weight. Pushing me down. 

The kettle whistles. 

I flinch so hard my hip slams into the drawer. The sound rings in my ears. I grab the handle, my heart hammering a frantic, sick rhythm against the heavy thing in my chest. I pour the water. Chamomile for Maya. Earl Grey for me.

"Morning."

Maya is in the doorway. She looks sleepy. Her hair is messy. I love her so much it physically aches; I have loved her since we were twelve years old eating pizza rolls on her basement floor.

"Morning," I say. 

My voice sounds perfectly normal. It terrifies me how normal it sounds. 

"Tea’s ready," I say.

"You're an angel," she mumbles, shuffling over. She reaches for the yellow mug. As she takes it, her fingers brush the back of my hand.

I pull back. I can't help it. My body does it before my brain can stop it. I jerk my hand away so fast the tea sloshes over the rim of the mug and burns my knuckles.

Maya freezes. She looks at my hand. Then she looks at my face.

The silence stretches. It’s thick. It’s awful.

"Ry?" she says softly.

"Sorry," I say quickly. I grab a paper towel. I wipe the counter. I wipe the side of the mug. I don't look at her. "Static shock. You know. Dry air."

She doesn’t move. She just watches me wipe the counter. I can feel her looking at me. She has been looking at me like this for three weeks. I catch her from across rooms. Her eyes are full of this awful, helpless grief. She knows something is broken, but she doesn't know what, and she is terrified to touch it in case it falls apart completely.

So she takes the mug. "Thanks, Ry."

"Of course."

I smile at her. It uses every muscle in my face.

It's 8:04 AM. Chloe texts back: *omg u remembered! thank u literally crying rn 😭*

I heart the message.

Later, it's evening. The Whitmore group is coming over. 

I make a grocery run. I buy the cheddar popcorn David likes. I buy the exact brand of seltzer Sarah drinks. I carry the bags up the stairs. My legs feel like lead. I am so tired. I sleep for nine hours and wake up exhausted. I wake up in the middle of the night remembering the feeling of breath on my neck and I go to the bathroom and dry-heave until my throat is raw.

I arrange the snacks in bowls. I put a soft, knit blanket over the back of the sofa. I turn off the harsh overhead light. I turn on the three floor lamps. 

Warm light. Amber light. Safe.

They arrive. They laugh. They drop their coats on my bed. I queue up a playlist. Mellow indie, warm acoustics, seamless.

"Dude, the vibes in here are always so immaculate," David says, sinking into the sofa and grabbing a handful of popcorn. "I swear my blood pressure drops ten points when I walk in."

"I aim to please," I say.

I sit in the armchair. I pull my knees to my chest. I wrap my arms around my shins.

They are talking. Sarah is complaining about her lab partner. Maya is laughing. The room is glowing. 

I am not in the room.

I am back in that dark bedroom at the party, staring up at a ceiling I don't recognize. The bruising grip on my hips. The absolute, paralyzing realization that my voice did not matter. That I was just a thing. A warm thing to be used.

I look at my hands, wrapped around my knees. They don't feel like my hands. I feel like I am floating somewhere near the ceiling, looking down at a girl named Riley. 

I watch David’s cup. When it gets low, I get up and hand him a new seltzer. He doesn't even have to ask.

"Thanks, Ry."

"Gotcha."

I sit back down. Nobody knows. I am screaming so loud my throat is bleeding, but the acoustics in the room are perfect, the lighting is flawless, the popcorn is exactly right.

Sunday.

The phone buzzes on my nightstand. 

Mom.

I stare at it. It vibrates against the wood. I have to answer. I call her every Sunday. If I don't answer, she will worry. 

I clear my throat. I swallow down the dry, jagged feeling. 

"Hi, Mom."

"Hi, sweetie! How was your week?"

"Good," I say. I am lying flat on my back. I have the heavy quilt pulled up to my chin. I used to love the weight of it. Now it feels like a body pinning me down. I push it off. The cold air hits my sweat-damp shirt and I start shivering. I pull the quilt back up. "Just busy. Classes."

"You sound a little quiet, honey. Are you getting enough sleep?"

"Yeah. Just studying a lot."

"Well, make sure you take a break, okay? Have you seen Maya?"

"She's right in the living room."

"Good. Tell her I say hi. And Riley?"

"Yeah?"

"I love you."

I squeeze my eyes shut. A tear slips out. It runs hot into my hair. It is the only warm thing inside me.

"I love you too, Mom."

I hang up. I hold the phone to my chest.

I want my mom. I want her to drive the three hours from home and sit on my bed and brush my hair away from my forehead. But if she comes, she will look at me, and she will see it. Mothers always see it. And if I say it out loud, it becomes real. If I say it out loud, I will ruin her too.

I can't ruin them. I am the one who makes things nice. 

So I hold it. I pack it down tight, underneath the warmth, underneath the playlists and the tea and the soft blankets.

I get out of bed. I walk into the living room.

Maya is studying on the couch. The afternoon light from the window is hitting her laptop screen, making a harsh glare.

I reach over and adjust the blinds. The glare disappears.

She looks up at me. 

"Better?" I ask.

"Yeah," she whispers. "Better."

I go to the kitchen. I check the water pitcher. It's almost empty. I stand at the sink. I turn on the tap. I fill it.
